summary: A Forbes profile on Diane Hendricks, America's most successful self-made female entrepreneur and co-founder of ABC Supply. The article highlights her deep patriotism, relentless work ethic, and counter-cyclical business acquisitions following the death of her husband and the 2008 financial crisis.

principles.statement: Work provides ongoing purpose, regardless of your age.
principles.rationale: She continues to work long hours into her 70s because the ability to think and contribute gives her life meaning.
principles.source_quote: I am so frigging old and still going to work because I still can think. I feel like I add purpose.

principles.statement: Do whatever is necessary to survive and provide for yourself.
principles.rationale: Reflecting on her early years as a single teen mother working unconventional jobs to pay the bills.
principles.source_quote: You gotta do what you gotta do.

principles.statement: America offers boundless opportunity if you refuse to see closed doors.
principles.rationale: She attributes her success to the American Dream, never allowing her gender or background to act as perceived barriers.
principles.source_quote: I never had a door that didn’t open for me. I never thought about being a female and not being able to do what I do.

mental_models.name: Counter-Cyclical Expansion
mental_models.description: Viewing industry turmoil and market collapses as prime opportunities to acquire rivals at fire-sale prices.
mental_models.example: Orchestrating ABC Supply's biggest acquisition by buying rival Bradco in 2010 during the real estate market collapse.

heuristics: Provide an unheard-of level of customer service in notoriously unfriendly industries.

signature_quotes.text: I never had a door that didn’t open for me. I never thought about being a female and not being able to do what I do.
signature_quotes.context: On her belief in the American Dream and her experience as a female entrepreneur.

signature_quotes.text: It used to be that a job was a gift. You were proud.
signature_quotes.context: On the modern workforce's lack of appreciation for employment.

signature_quotes.text: I am so frigging old and still going to work because I still can think. I feel like I add purpose.
signature_quotes.context: On why she continues to wake up at 5 a.m. to work at age 75.

signature_quotes.text: That’s the most frustrating part of getting old. Golly, there’s still so much—so much to do.
signature_quotes.context: On the limits of time and her ongoing ambitions.

anti_patterns.description: Taking employment for granted and failing to appreciate having a job.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It reflects a loss of pride and work ethic; she believes a job should be treated as a 'gift' to take pride in.
